refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and the system to achieve and maintain energy efficiency performance such that measured energy_per_ton (energy_used / ton_moved) remains at or below the customer-defined E_target throughout the evaluation period, under nominal operating conditions and across the range of supported worksite scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-003 is a high-level KPI requirement that must be decomposed into measurable, controllable functional requirements. The refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is a system-level outcome dependent on multiple subsystems (propulsion, hydraulics, teleoperation overhead, idle management, load optimization). The requirement now explicitly bounds the evaluation context (nominal conditions, evaluation period, worksite scenarios) to enable testability and to distinguish between design-controllable factors and operational/environmental factors.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Establish a baseline energy consumption model (kWh per ton) under controlled worksite conditions. Instrument the vehicle with power metering at major subsystems (engine, hydraulic pump, electric drivetrain, teleoperation link). Conduct acceptance testing over a representative mission profile. Compare measured energy_per_ton against E_target. Document any deviations and root causes (e.g., operator technique, terrain, load variability).
sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The propulsion and drivetrain subsystem shall minimize energy loss during power transmission and load movement.
FR-EE-002: The hydraulic and actuation subsystem shall minimize parasitic losses and idle consumption.
FR-EE-003: The teleoperation subsystem shall minimize communication and processing overhead energy consumption.
FR-EE-004: The vehicle control system shall optimize load scheduling and idle state management to reduce unnecessary energy expenditure.
FR-EE-005: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time energy consumption visibility and advisory feedback to operators to support energy-aware decision-making.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a multi-layer energy optimization architecture comprising efficient drivetrain design (low-loss transmission, regenerative braking where applicable), hydraulic system efficiency improvements (variable displacement pump, load-sensing controls), teleoperation bandwidth optimization (compression, selective telemetry), and operator feedback dashboards.

DP-B: Adopt a centralized energy management controller that monitors real-time power draw across all subsystems, enforces dynamic power budgeting policies, and throttles non-critical functions (e.g., auxiliary systems, high-bandwidth telemetry) when energy consumption approaches a threshold.

DP-C: Implement a hybrid approach combining efficient hardware design (DP-A) with intelligent energy management software (DP-B), supported by operator training and worksite energy-aware operational procedures.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Implement a hybrid approach combining efficient hardware design (low-loss drivetrain, variable displacement hydraulics, teleoperation bandwidth optimization) with intelligent energy management software (real-time power monitoring, dynamic load scheduling, operator feedback dashboards), supported by operator training and worksite energy-aware operational procedures.

potential_sources: Coupling between drivetrain efficiency (mechanical design) and energy management controller (software) is managed via well-defined power telemetry interfaces and control commands.
Coupling between teleoperation bandwidth optimization and operator experience is mitigated by maintaining minimum acceptable latency and video quality thresholds.
Coupling between energy management policies and operator workload is addressed through clear feedback mechanisms and training.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C avoids over-coupling by separating concerns: hardware efficiency is a passive enabler, software management is active control, and operator procedures provide the human-in-the-loop governance. No single subsystem is solely responsible for energy efficiency; the outcome is emergent from the integrated system.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Operators will adopt energy-aware procedures and respond to feedback; training and incentive structures are in place.
ASSUMPTION: Teleoperation bandwidth can be optimized without degrading safety-critical control latency or situational awareness.
ASSUMPTION: Hardware efficiency improvements (drivetrain, hydraulics) are cost-effective and do not introduce unacceptable reliability or maintenance burdens.
RISK: If operator training is inadequate or incentives are misaligned, energy efficiency gains may not be realized in practice.
RISK: Aggressive energy management policies (e.g., throttling auxiliary systems) may conflict with safety or comfort requirements under certain conditions.
RISK: Teleoperation bandwidth optimization may introduce latency spikes or video artifacts that degrade operator confidence and increase accident risk.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly define energy management policy guardrails: (1) safety-critical functions (steering, braking, emergency stop) are never throttled; (2) operator situational awareness (video, audio, haptic feedback) maintains minimum quality thresholds; (3) energy optimization is applied only to non-critical auxiliary systems and load scheduling. Add a requirement for operator acceptance testing and feedback before deployment.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level system boundary encompassing all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and their integration. Energy efficiency is a system-level KPI that emerges from the coordinated operation of all subsystems. The NGHE Solution IM is responsible for defining energy efficiency targets, allocating energy budgets to subsystems, integrating energy telemetry and feedback, and ensuring that operational procedures and training support energy-aware behavior.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Energy consumption data (kWh, power draw by subsystem, energy_per_ton metric) is transmitted from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to the NGHE Solution integration layer and onward to Site Manager dashboards and analytics systems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Mission and load scheduling directives from Site Manager inform energy optimization decisions; the NGHE Solution translates tasking into energy-aware load plans and operator guidance.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Energy management policies must not compromise safety; SafetyAlert signals from Safety Services override energy optimization actions (e.g., throttling is suspended during emergency maneuvers).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation and remote monitoring rely on Network Link; bandwidth optimization for energy efficiency must maintain minimum latency and reliability for safety-critical control.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural system-level container for energy efficiency KPI management. It owns the energy target definition, subsystem budget allocation, integration of telemetry, and operational procedures. Minimal diffusion: energy efficiency is a cross-cutting concern, but the NGHE Solution IM provides the governance and integration point.

Vehicle contains the hardware efficiency improvements (drivetrain, hydraulics) and vehicle-level energy monitoring. However, energy efficiency is not solely a Vehicle concern; it depends on Teleoperation overhead, Safety Services overhead, and operator behavior. Assigning the entire FR to Vehicle would create diffusion across subsystems and obscure system-level optimization.

Teleoperation contributes to energy efficiency through bandwidth optimization and communication overhead reduction. However, Teleoperation is a subsystem; energy efficiency is a system-level KPI. Assigning the FR to Teleoperation alone would miss hardware and operational factors.

Safety Services provides constraints and guardrails for energy management (e.g., safety-critical functions are never throttled). Safety Services does not own energy efficiency; it owns safety. Assigning the FR to Safety Services would be a category error.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Energy efficiency (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is a system-level KPI that emerges from the integrated operation of Vehicle (hardware efficiency), Teleoperation (communication efficiency), Safety Services (safety constraints), and operator behavior (energy-aware procedures). The NGHE Solution IM is the appropriate owner because it: (1) defines the energy target and acceptance criteria; (2) allocates energy budgets to subsystems; (3) integrates energy telemetry from all subsystems; (4) enforces energy management policies subject to safety constraints; (5) provides operator feedback and training. Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are responsible for their respective contributions to energy efficiency, but the NGHE Solution IM orchestrates the system-level outcome.
containment_risks: RISK: If energy management policies are defined at the NGHE Solution level without sufficient detail, subsystems may lack clear guidance on energy budgets and optimization priorities.
RISK: If energy telemetry integration is incomplete or delayed, the NGHE Solution may not have visibility into actual energy consumption and may fail to detect deviations from E_target.
RISK: If operator training and procedures are not aligned with energy management policies, operators may inadvertently circumvent energy optimization or create safety conflicts.
RISK: If energy efficiency is treated as a NGHE Solution-level concern without clear allocation to subsystems, accountability for energy performance may be diffuse and difficult to trace.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish a clear energy budget allocation framework: (1) NGHE Solution defines the overall energy_per_ton target and acceptance criteria; (2) Vehicle is allocated a maximum energy budget for propulsion, hydraulics, and vehicle-level systems; (3) Teleoperation is allocated a maximum energy budget for communication and remote control overhead; (4) Safety Services is allocated a maximum energy budget for safety monitoring and alerting; (5) NGHE Solution integration layer monitors total energy consumption and enforces policies to stay within budget. Create explicit energy telemetry contracts between subsystems and the NGHE Solution IM to ensure visibility and accountability.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The refined FR (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target under nominal conditions) is directly addressed by DP-C (hybrid hardware + software + procedures approach). The DP is feasible, testable, and aligns with the FR's intent. The coupling check confirms that the DP does not introduce unacceptable interdependencies. The DP is appropriately scoped to the system level and does not over-constrain subsystem design.

diffusion_risks: Energy efficiency is a cross-cutting concern that touches all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). The NGHE Solution IM must provide clear governance and integration mechanisms to avoid diffusion of responsibility.

Operator behavior (training, procedures) is a critical factor in energy efficiency but is difficult to control and measure. The NGHE Solution IM must establish clear feedback mechanisms and incentive structures to align operator behavior with energy targets.
Energy management policies may conflict with safety, comfort, or operational flexibility. The NGHE Solution IM must define clear policy guardrails and escalation procedures to resolve conflicts.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: The NGHE Solution IM is the appropriate container for energy efficiency KPI management because it owns the system-level target, budget allocation, telemetry integration, and policy enforcement. Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are responsible for their respective contributions, but the NGHE Solution IM orchestrates the system-level outcome. This containment strategy minimizes diffusion by centralizing governance while allowing subsystems to optimize their own energy performance within allocated budgets.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: The refined FR is verifiable through instrumentation of the vehicle with power metering at major subsystems, controlled testing under representative mission profiles, and comparison of measured energy_per_ton against E_target. Acceptance testing should include both nominal conditions and edge cases (e.g., difficult terrain, heavy loads, extended teleoperation). Operator feedback and training effectiveness should be assessed through post-deployment monitoring.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of energy efficiency responsibility is clear: NGHE Solution owns the system-level KPI and integration; Vehicle owns hardware efficiency; Teleoperation owns communication efficiency; Safety Services owns safety constraints; operators own energy-aware behavior. Each subsystem has a defined role and measurable contribution to the overall energy efficiency target.

recommended_revisions: Refine the energy budget allocation framework to specify maximum energy budgets for each subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and define how budgets are monitored and enforced.
Establish energy telemetry contracts between subsystems and the NGHE Solution IM to ensure visibility, consistency, and accountability.
Define energy management policy guardrails to protect safety-critical functions and operator situational awareness from energy optimization actions.
Develop operator training and procedures to support energy-aware behavior and establish feedback mechanisms to reinforce energy efficiency goals.
Plan for post-deployment monitoring and continuous improvement of energy efficiency performance based on field data.

decision.justification: The refined FR (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is a system-level KPI that is not atomic and requires further decomposition into subsystem-level functional requirements. The five sub-FRs identified (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-005) address distinct subsystems and concerns: propulsion efficiency, hydraulic efficiency, teleoperation efficiency, load scheduling, and operator feedback. Each sub-FR is more atomic and can be assigned to a specific subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) for detailed design. The NGHE Solution IM provides the integration and governance layer, but subsystem-level design decisions must be made at the next decomposition level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The propulsion and drivetrain subsystem shall minimize energy loss during power transmission and load movement.
FR-EE-002: The hydraulic and actuation subsystem shall minimize parasitic losses and idle consumption.
FR-EE-003: The teleoperation subsystem shall minimize communication and processing overhead energy consumption.
FR-EE-004: The vehicle control system shall optimize load scheduling and idle state management to reduce unnecessary energy expenditure.
FR-EE-005: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time energy consumption visibility and advisory feedback to operators to support energy-aware decision-making.

assumptions: Operators will adopt energy-aware procedures and respond to feedback; training and incentive structures are in place.
Teleoperation bandwidth can be optimized without degrading safety-critical control latency or situational awareness.
Hardware efficiency improvements (drivetrain, hydraulics) are cost-effective and do not introduce unacceptable reliability or maintenance burdens.
Energy efficiency is measured over a representative evaluation period under nominal operating conditions; environmental and operational variability is documented and accounted for.
The customer-defined E_target is achievable with the selected DP-C approach and is aligned with industry benchmarks and cost-benefit analysis.
open_risks: If operator training is inadequate or incentives are misaligned, energy efficiency gains may not be realized in practice.
Aggressive energy management policies may conflict with safety or comfort requirements under certain conditions.
Teleoperation bandwidth optimization may introduce latency spikes or video artifacts that degrade operator confidence and increase accident risk.
Energy efficiency performance may vary significantly across different worksite scenarios, terrain types, and load profiles; acceptance testing must be representative.
Post-deployment energy efficiency may degrade due to wear, maintenance issues, or operator behavior drift; continuous monitoring and improvement mechanisms are needed.
evidence_needed: Baseline energy consumption model for the NGHE solution under controlled conditions.
Detailed design specifications for hardware efficiency improvements (drivetrain, hydraulics) with energy impact estimates.
Teleoperation bandwidth optimization analysis with latency and quality impact assessment.
Energy management policy specifications with safety guardrails and conflict resolution procedures.
Operator training and procedures documentation with effectiveness metrics.
Acceptance test plan with representative mission profiles, measurement methodology, and acceptance criteria.
Post-deployment monitoring plan with energy consumption tracking, anomaly detection, and continuous improvement mechanisms.
